title: 第 35 節
en: ' With the following verse, Rv. X. 18, 11, he should throw earth (into the pit) :
' 0 Earth, open wide, do not press him, be kind in admitting, and in embracing him, cover him, 0 Earth, as a mother covers her son with her cloth.'
' After he has thrown the earth, he should recite the next verse, Rv. X. 18, 12 :
' May the opened earth stand firm, and may a thousand supports stand near : may these dwellings be running with ghnta offerings, and may there always be safety for him there.'
' He then covers (the urn) with a lid, saying, Rv. X.
' I raise up the earth all around thee, may I not hurt thee in putting down this slab. May the Pit?/s hold this cairn of thine, may Yama there make seats for thee.'
'Then returning without looking back, and after bathing, they should give him the iS'raddha offering^.'
The actual burial is now finished, and it is clear that the Vedic Indians both burnt and buried their dead. The urn into which the bones were deposited ^ See Appendix VI, Note 4.
FUNEKAL CEREMONIES. 257 must have been large, and it is strange that none of them, so far as I know, should ever have been discovered.

The honours to be paid to the dead do not cease, however, with the burial. We saw that at the end of it a /Sraddha offering was to be made. These offerings, meant for the deceased, are continued afterwards during a whole year. After that time they cease to be addressed to him individually, he having taken his place among the three ancestors, father, grandfather, and great-grandfather, and receiving the common >Sraddhas at the times appointed \ Before, however, we proceed to consider this important branch of the Indian religion, the /S'raddha, Ave have still to follow the mourners to a kind of expiatory service, which was meant to pacify death, and to guard the survivors against every evil.

' They who have lost a Guru (father, mother, or teacher), or have been afflicted in any other way, should on the day of the new moon perform an expiatory service. Other authorities fix on the ninth day after death, i.e. on the tenth day, for this ceremony. Before sunrise they should carry the fire and ashes in a receptacle southward, saying, Rv. X. 16, 9 :
' Agni, the flesh-eater, I send away far ; may he, the evil-bringer, go to those who own Yama as their king.'

' They then throw the fire away where four roads meet, or somewhere else, and walk three times round it, turning their left to it, and striking their left thighs with their left hands. They then return home without looking back, wash, have their hair, their ^ On the Sraddhas, see India, What can it tmch us? pp. 234-242, (3 S 258 LECTURE IX.

beard, the hair of the body, and their nails cut, and procure for themselves new jars, pots, and vessels for rinsing, adorned with wreaths of >S'ami flowers, fuel of iS'ami wood, two pieces for kindling fire and (fifteen) for laying round it, also dung of bulls, a hide, fresh butter, a stone, and as many bunches of Kusa grass as there are young women (in the house). Then at the time of the Agnihotra (in the afternoon) he (the oldest person present) should kindle the fire, saying, Rv. X. 16, 9 :
• But here may this other (Agni) Gatavedas, cany the sacrifice to the gods, knowing (how to do it).'
' Keeping that fire burning they sit until the still night, repeating the tales of the old, and having sacred stories, such as Itihasas and Purartas, recited to them.

' When all sounds have ceased, and when (others) have gone to their house or resting-place, he (the performer), stepping from the south side of the door to the north, should pour out a continuous stream of water, reciting a verse (Rv. X. 16, 9).

' Having then lit a fire (aupasana), he spreads west of it the hide of a bull, its neck turned to the east, and the hair outside, and asks the people of the house (amatya), to step on it, saying, Rv. X. J 8, 6 :
as you are, one after the other. Tvash/n' who grants good offspring may graciously yield you here a long life to live.'
' He should then place logs round the fii'e, the first with the words, Rv. X. 18, 4:
to that place ; may they live through full a hundred harvests, hiding death with this stone.'
'While he says, " hiding death with this stone," he FUNEEA.L CEREMONIES. 259 places a stone north of the fire. He then recites four verses, and sacrifices with each, Rv. X. 18, 1 :

from the way of the gods. I speak to tliee who hast eyes to see and ears to hear, do not hurt our offspring, or our men ! * ' Be pure and clean, ye who are partakers in the sacrifice, so that, effacing the track of death, ye may continue a longer life, abounding in offspring and wealth.'
'Then follow two verses, translated before. He should then look at his people (amatya) and recite, Rv. X. 18, 5 :
' As days follow one another, as seasons go rightly with seasons, so shape our lives, O Creator, that the young may not leave the old behind.'

' The young women should then with some young Darbha-blades take fresh butter, and anoint their eyes with the thumbs and fore-fingers of both hands (at the same time) ; and after that turn round and throw (the blades) away. While they are anointing themselves, the performer should look at them and say, Rv. X. 18, 7 :
'These women, not widows, but having good husbands, may anoint themselves with collyi'ium and butter. Without tears, without ailments, and well adorned, may the wives go first to the house ^'

This has indeed proved a fatal verse to thousands of women who have been burnt on the pile with their husbands, on the strength of a spurious reading. ' They should go fii'st to the house ' is in the text yonim agre. By changing agre into agneA, the line would mean, ' They should go into the house, or into the lap of Agni (fire).' This reading was foisted into the Veda, and after that, widow-burning was represented as having a sacred authority in the Veda.

^ For other readings of this verse, see Rajendralal Mitra, I. c, p. 52. The Taitt. Ar., VI. 10, 8, reads ^iiganena, sarpisha sam mrtsantam, which Rajendralal Mitra translates, ' let the women smear themselves with collyrious butter.'
' Then the performer should first touch the stone, (the others after him), saying, Rv. X. 53, 8 :
zh: 用下列詩句，梨俱吠陀第十卷第十八讚，第十一頌，他應當將土地（投入坑中）：
「啊，大地呀，開啟寬廣之門，莫要壓迫他，在接納他時要溫柔，在擁抱他時要溫柔，請覆蓋他吧，啊，大地呀，如同母親用衣布覆蓋其子一般。」
拋擲泥土後，他應誦念下一首詩頌，梨俱吠陀第十卷第十八讚歌第十二節：
'願大地展開而堅立，願千般支柱立於近處；願這些住所充滿酥油（ghṛta）的供奉，願他在那裡永遠得享安全。'
他隨後用蓋子蓋住（骨灰罐），並念誦梨俱吠陀第十卷。
「我在你周圍高舉大地，願我在放下這塊石板時不會傷害你。願地坑容納你的石堆，願閻摩在那裡為你準備座位。」
「隨後返回時不回頭張望，沐浴後，他們應當供奉他 iS'raddha 祭品。」
實際的埋葬現已完成，由此可明確看出吠陀時代的印度人既焚燒亦埋葬他們的亡者。用以安置骨骸的骨灰罐 ^ 參見附錄六，註4。
葬禮儀式。257 必然規模宏大，令人奇異的是，據我所知，迄今未曾發現其中任何一座。

對亡靈所應當獻上的敬禮並不因埋葬而終止。我們已經看到在葬禮結束時必須舉行一次吒剌陀（Sraddha）祭祀。這些為亡者準備的祭祀在其後會持續整整一年。在此期間之後，這些祭祀便不再個別地獻給他，因為他已經進入三位祖先——父親、祖父和曾祖父——的行列中，並於指定的時刻接受共同的吒剌陀祭祀。然而，在我們進行探討這一印度宗教的重要分支、吒剌陀祭祀之前，我們仍需跟隨喪禮者參與一種補贖性的儀式，其目的在於平息死神，並保護生存者免受一切禍害。
喪失上師（父親、母親或教師），或以任何其他方式遭受苦難者，應在新月之日舉行贖罪儀式。其他權威人士將此儀式定於死後第九天，即第十天舉行。日出前，他們應將火焰和骨灰裝在容器中向南方運送，並誦唸《梨俱吠陀》X. 16, 9：
「我遣送食肉者火神阿耆尼遠去；願此惡帶來者歸往以閻摩為王者之地。」
他們隨後會把火丟棄在四條道路相交之處，或其他某個地方，然後繞火走三圈，使左邊朝向火，並用左手擊打自己的左大腿。之後他們回家時不回頭張望，洗淨身體，整理頭髮，他們的^ 關於施身祭（Sraddhas），見《印度，它能教給我們什麼？》第234-242頁。（3 S 258 第九講。

鬍鬚、身體的毛髮與指甲都要剪去，並為自己置備新的罐子、瓶子與沐浴用的容器，以薩彌（S'ami）花冠作裝飾，薩彌木作燃料，兩根生火用的木棍與（十五根）排列在周圍用的木棍，還有公牛的糞便、獸皮、新鮮的奶油、石頭，以及與家中年輕女性數量相同的多束庫沙草（Kusa grass）。隨後在晡時祭火儀式（Agnihotra）的時刻，他（在場的最年長者）應當點燃火焰，並誦念《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第16讚第9句（Rv. X. 16, 9）：
但此處（火神阿格尼）《知識吠陀》可將祭祀傳達諸神，知悉（如何為之）。
他們保持那火焰燃燒，坐著直到夜深人靜，重複講述古人的故事，並聽誦神聖的敘事文獻，例如《往世書》（Itihasa）和《往世部書》（Purana）。
當所有聲音都已停止，且（他人）已各自前往其住所或休息之處時，他（表演者）應從門的南側踏向北側，邊誦唸詩句（梨俱吠陀 X. 16, 9），邊傾注連續不斷的水流。
點燃火焰（aupasana）後，他在火的西側鋪開一張牛皮，牛皮的頸部轉向東方，毛髮朝外，並要求家中的人（amatya）踩踏其上，言辭如《梨俱吠陀》十卷十八讚六頌所述：
像你們那樣，一個接著一個。特瓦什特利（Tvash/n'）——賜予良好後代者——願在此慈悲地賦予你們長壽以安享人生。
他應當在火堆周圍放置木柴，第一塊時應誦唸梨俱吠陀第十卷第十八讚第四節（Rv. X. 18, 4）的言辭：
到那個地方；願他們活過整整一百個收穫季，用這塊石頭隱藏死亡。'
當他說著「用這塊石頭隱藏死亡」時，他在火的北邊放置一塊石頭。然後他誦唸四段讚頌詩，並依次獻祭。《梨俱吠陀》X. 18, 1：
來自神靈之道。我向你們這些有眼能見、有耳能聞者說話，莫傷害我們的子孫或我們的人民！*『你們這些參與祭祀者，要保持純淨和清潔，如此方能消除死亡的蹤跡，你們才能延續更長的生命，子孫眾多、財富豐盛。』
然後依次出現兩首詩句，前文已譯過。他應當此時審視其人民（amatya），並誦讀《梨俱吠陀》十卷十八讚歌第五詩節：
「如日子相繼而至，如四時循序相隨，願創造者啊，塑造我們的生命，使年輕的不致將年邁的遠遠拋在身後。」
年輕婦女應當用一些年輕的草葉（Darbha-blades）沾取新鮮的牛油，用雙手的拇指和食指同時塗抹她們的眼睛；之後轉身將草葉丟棄。當她們在塗抹自己時，施行者應當注視她們，並誦念《梨俱吠陀》第十卷第18讚第7節（Rv. X. 18, 7）：
「這些婦女，並非寡婦，而是有良夫相伴者，得以用眼線膏（collyi'ium）及奶油為自身塗抹。無淚無疾，妝飾得宜，妻子們得以率先前往那家園^」
這確實對數千名婦女造成了致命的影響，她們因著一個偽造的讀本而被與丈夫一同焚燒於火堆上。「她們應當先往屋舍」在經文中是 yonim agre。若將 agre 改為 agneA，該行便會意為「她們應當進入屋舍，或進入阿格尼（火）的懷抱」。這個讀本被偽造地插入了吠陀，之後，寡婦殉夫便被聲稱在吠陀中具有神聖的權威。
^ 關於此詩節的其他釋讀，見拉真德拉‧米特拉（Rajendralal Mitra）著，前引書第52頁。《泰替里耶本集》（Taitt. Ar.）VI. 10, 8 讀作 ^iiganena, sarpisha sam mrtsantam，拉真德拉‧米特拉譯為「讓婦女們以香油塗抹自身」（let the women smear themselves with collyrious butter）。
然後執行者應首先觸摸石頭（其他人隨後），說梨俱吠陀第十卷第五十三讚第八頌：
